🟡 Bioregional Learning Centre South Devon

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Bioregional Learning Centre (BLC) in South Devon works at the intersection of geosystems, human systems, and ecosystems to build long-term climate resilience and civic capacity. Its work is structured around four key pillars– developing place-based funding ecosystems; enabling citizen-led data collection and analysis to track vitality and adaptive capacity; supporting community groups with evidence for effective representation in governance; and fostering systems thinking through education and cultural engagement.

Bioregional Context

The region is defined by five rivers, with the River Tamar in the west (serving as the historic 7th century Cornwall-Devon boundary) and River Tean in the east. All rivers rise from Dartmoor in the north, flowing to the English Channel in the south. They recognize potential connections to a wider "Channel bioregion," noting stronger commonalities with French coastal communities than northern neighbors. The landscape is characterized by multiple river valleys that shape both the physical environment and human settlement patterns.

Place
River Dart, Greenway Road, Brixham, TQ5 0, United Kingdom
links
https://youtu.be/m47HX3U2Auo|https://devondoughnut.org/

Tags

👁️‍🗨️ Storying_Place 🍩 Doughnut_Assessment 💧 Watershed_Repair 🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science 🦏 Rights_of_Nature 🤝🏾 Agreements_and_Legal_Instruments 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling 🧳 Immersive_Journeys 🎒 Learning_Programs

Background


The Bioregional Learning Centre (BLC) in South Devon operates in a region defined by five rivers flowing from Dartmoor in the north to the English Channel in the south. The River Tamar in the west has served as the historic boundary between Cornwall and Devon since the 7th century, while the River Teign marks the eastern boundary.

Origins & Network


The Bioregional Learning Centre prioritizes relationships in how they describe and work with bioregions—focusing on the ability to build relationships, create connective tissue, and understand natural boundaries. They work at the intersection of geosystems, human systems, and ecosystems, understanding how living systems are changing, and how to adapt.

Philosophy & Approach


The organization has identified four foundational pillars necessary to sustain their work long-term:

1. Finance: Developing place-based funding ecosystems for bioregional work.
2. Data and Metrics: Enabling citizen-led data collection, use, access, sharing, and interrogation. .
3. Governance: Supporting community groups with solid information and evidence for effective representation.
4. Arts and Culture: Using creative engagement to connect communities with place and ecological issues they cannot directly experience.

Together, these pillars form resilient infrastructure: data informs governance, which strengthens community agency, and attracts contextually appropriate funding.

Activities

🔸

Story of Place

 🔸

Devon Doughnut Project

 🔸

Dartington Charter & Dance

 🔸

Dancing in Salt Marshes

 🔸

Climate Adaptation Learning Journey

Vision and Legacy


The BLC has been doing extensive projects, particularly on rivers, engaging local people in thinking about drought, water conservation, salt marsh restoration, and river charters. Increasingly, they recognize the need to put a platform or pillars underneath their work to sustain it long-term—the four pillars of finance, data and metrics, governance, and arts and culture. This approach has produced tangible outcomes—from river charter co-development and Devon Doughnut economic frameworks to regional ecosystem interventions and innovative arts-based engagement connecting communities with place.
